_purpose: Life Pattern for the Appalachian subsistence squeeze, 1880-1940. The multi-generational arc of mountain families facing declining soil, subdivided land, timber and coal company encroachment, and the slow impoverishment that pushed children out of the hollows one at a time.
short_description: Mountain families who had farmed the same hollows for three or more generations faced a slow crisis between 1880 and 1940. Land was subdivided past viability through partible inheritance. Soil thinned on steep hillside plots never meant for permanent cultivation. Timber companies bought ridgeline tracts and closed the open range that sustained free-roaming hogs. Coal companies recruited men into company towns that offered cash wages but ended subsistence independence. The squeeze was not a single event but a decades-long compression — drought, illness, debt, and the chestnut blight each tightened the vise. Children left one at a time: first to the coal camps a few ridges over, then to the factory cities of Ohio and Michigan.
regions: Eastern Kentucky (Perry, Knott, Letcher, Harlan, Pike counties)
West Virginia coalfields (McDowell, Mingo, Logan, Raleigh counties)
Western North Carolina mountains (Buncombe, Madison, Mitchell, Avery counties)
Eastern Tennessee (Campbell, Claiborne, Scott, Morgan counties)
population: Scots-Irish and English-descent families who had farmed mountain hollows since the late eighteenth or early nineteenth century. Typically three to five generations deep by 1880. Small-farm families cultivating 10-50 acres of steep hillside, supplementing crops with hunting, gathering, and open-range livestock. Average family size six to ten children. By 1900, many families occupied holdings too small for subsistence, and the men had begun seasonal wage work in timber and coal operations while women and children maintained the home place.

lifecycle_phases.description: The mountain farm family exists in a fragile equilibrium. The farm is small — 10 to 40 acres of cultivable hillside, with access to open-range forest for hog grazing, chestnut gathering, and hunting. Corn is the staple crop, supplemented by beans, potatoes, and a kitchen garden. Hogs fatten on forest mast (chestnuts, acorns, hickory nuts) and are driven to market over mountain trails. The family makes most of what it needs: clothing from homespun, tools from local iron, furniture from local timber. Cash enters the economy through occasional surplus sales and seasonal labor — floating timber, hauling supplies, working in small local sawmills. The equilibrium depends on access to the commons — the unenclosed forest land that provides mast, game, and grazing. When that access is disrupted, the squeeze begins.
record_signatures: County tax rolls showing small acreage holdings (10-40 acres)
Census records listing occupation as 'farmer' with no change across decades
Deed books showing family land held since the early 1800s
County court records — livestock mark registrations, open-range disputes
lifecycle_phases.narrative_hook: In {year}, the {family_name} family was still farming the same hollow in {origin_county} that {pronoun_possessive} great-grandfather had settled — {number} acres of hillside corn, a garden, hogs running in the chestnut woods, and a way of life that had sustained five generations on land the lowlanders would have called worthless.

lifecycle_phases.description: External forces begin closing the commons. Timber companies buy ridgeline tracts and mineral rights from absentee landowners or directly from families who do not fully understand the 'broad form deed' that signs away subsurface rights while nominally retaining surface ownership. The open range is fenced or logged, eliminating the forest mast that sustained free-roaming hogs. The American chestnut blight, arriving around 1904 and spreading through Appalachia over the next three decades, destroys the single most important wild food source — chestnuts provided calories for humans and the mast that fattened hogs for market. Meanwhile, land subdivision continues: a 40-acre farm divided among four sons produces 10-acre holdings, each too small for self-sufficiency. The equilibrium is breaking, but the family has no single dramatic moment of crisis — only a gradual tightening.
record_signatures: Deed books showing mineral rights transfers ('broad form deeds')
Census records showing occupational shift: 'farmer' → 'farmer and laborer'
County agricultural reports noting declining crop yields
Deed books showing subdivision of family tracts
lifecycle_phases.narrative_hook: The timber company bought the ridgeline in {year}, and suddenly the woods where {ancestor_name}'s hogs had always run were posted with 'No Trespass' signs — the mountain hadn't changed, but the rules had, and the family's margin of survival narrowed with every tree that fell.

lifecycle_phases.description: Men begin leaving the farm for seasonal wage work — in coal mines, sawmills, and timber operations — while women and children maintain the home place. The pattern is initially supplementary: a man works in the mines for three or four months in winter, sends cash home, and returns for planting season. But the cash economy is addictive and necessary. The family now needs money for goods it once made or grew: commercial flour replaces homegrown wheat, store-bought cloth replaces homespun, patent medicine replaces herbal remedies. The farm alone cannot provide these things, and the farm alone can no longer feed the family. The seasonal pattern gradually becomes permanent as men find year-round work in coal camps and sawmills. Some men move their families to company towns; others maintain the dual arrangement for years, the family split between hollow and camp.
record_signatures: Census records showing men listed as 'miner' or 'laborer' while family remains at farm address
County school records showing enrollment from both farm and mining-camp addresses
Store ledger books documenting shift from barter to cash purchases
lifecycle_phases.narrative_hook: By {decade}, {ancestor_name} was likely spending winters in the coal mines of {destination_city} and summers on the farm in {origin_county} — earning the cash the family needed to buy what the shrinking farm could no longer provide, and watching {pronoun_possessive} children grow up divided between two worlds.

lifecycle_phases.description: The squeeze reaches its conclusion. Children who grew up watching the farm shrink and the mines beckon make the final choice: the oldest sons may stay in the coal camps permanently, moving their families into company housing. Younger children, seeing the mines' dangers and the farm's decline, leave for factory cities — Cincinnati, Dayton, Detroit, Chicago, Akron. They follow kin networks: a cousin already working at a rubber plant in Akron sends word that there are jobs. The departure is not a dramatic exodus but a slow thinning. One child leaves, then another, then another, over the course of a decade. The home place becomes a summer-visit destination — the old couple maintains the garden and the cemetery, and the children return for funerals and holidays. When the last of the old generation dies, the farm is sold, abandoned, or reverts to forest. The hollow that held five generations holds none.
record_signatures: City directories in Cincinnati, Dayton, Detroit, Akron showing Appalachian birthplaces
Census records showing family members scattered across multiple states
Social Security applications documenting mountain birthplaces and industrial-city addresses
County deed books showing farm sales or tax-sale forfeitures
Marriage records in destination cities linking mountain-origin families
lifecycle_phases.narrative_hook: The children left {origin_county} the way the hollow emptied — not all at once but one at a time, first {name} to the coal camp, then {name} to the factory in {destination_city}, until the farm that had sustained five generations held only the old couple and the cemetery.

entry_conditions.description: Families whose ancestors settled Appalachian hollows during the Scots-Irish frontier migration of the late eighteenth century. By 1880, these families are three to five generations deep in the same hollow, having lived through the yeoman farming stability pattern for decades before the squeeze begins. The entry is not a migration but an inheritance — the squeeze descends on families who are already there.

Mountain families whose economic position was weakened by the Civil War — through property destruction, livestock confiscation, or the loss of men killed in service. The war accelerated the subdivision process by forcing premature estate divisions and disrupting the labor supply. Families that entered the postwar period with damaged farms and fewer working hands were more vulnerable to the squeeze that followed.

exit_conditions.description: Family members leave for industrial cities in Ohio, Michigan, and Indiana — the Appalachian out-migration that transforms Cincinnati, Dayton, Detroit, and Akron between 1920 and 1960. This is the primary exit path, carrying the largest number of people. The migration follows kinship networks: families from the same hollow cluster in the same urban neighborhoods, recreating mountain community structures in industrial cities.

Family members move permanently into coal company towns, exchanging farm subsistence for industrial wage labor. The move is typically short-distance — the coal camp may be only a few ridges from the home hollow — but the economic transformation is total. The family goes from self-directed subsistence to company-directed wage dependency: company housing, company store, company rules. This exit feeds into the company town lifecycle pattern.

Some families stay through the entire squeeze, adapting rather than leaving. They combine small-scale farming with wage work, government relief (after 1933), and informal economy. These families maintain the home place through the Depression, through the coal bust, and into the postwar era. Their persistence is not failure to leave but a choice — the attachment to place and kin outweighing the pull of industrial wages. These families appear in the record as continuous presence: the same surname on the tax rolls, the same family in the church, decade after decade.

daily_life.narrative: A mountain family's daily life in the squeeze era was defined by the gap between what the farm could produce and what the family needed. Mornings began before dawn — women lit fires, cooked cornbread and beans, and sent children to do chores. Men worked the hillside corn patch or, increasingly, walked or rode to a sawmill or mine for wage work. The garden provided beans, potatoes, cabbage, and greens. Hogs still roamed where the forest remained, but the range was shrinking. Women preserved food — drying beans and apples, canning, salting pork — with an intensity born of necessity. Sunday was church: the Old Regular Baptist or Missionary Baptist service was the week's social event, and the dinner-on-the-ground that followed was both fellowship and a shared meal that stretched every family's provisions.

details.description: Corn was the staple crop, grown on steep hillsides using a hoe rather than a plow — the slopes were too steep for animal-drawn equipment in many hollows. A family might cultivate 3-10 acres of corn, yielding 15-30 bushels per acre on good land. The corn was ground at a local water mill (toll: one-eighth of the grain) into meal for cornbread, the dietary foundation. By the early twentieth century, yields were declining as decades of hillside cultivation without terracing or fertilization eroded the thin topsoil. Gullies carved by rainwater on exposed slopes were visible evidence of the land's deterioration.
details.source: Eller, Ronald D. 'Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers: Industrialization of the Appalachian South, 1880-1930.' University of Tennessee Press, 1982, pp. 3-30.

details.description: The American chestnut had been the mountain family's most reliable wild resource. Chestnuts fed hogs, fed families, and provided the only reliable cash crop some families had — gathered chestnuts were sold by the bushel to dealers who shipped them to eastern cities. The chestnut blight (Cryphonectria parasitica), first observed in New York in 1904, reached central Appalachia by the 1910s and killed virtually every mature American chestnut by 1940. The loss of the chestnut eliminated a food source that had sustained the open-range hog economy, removed a cash-crop option, and reduced the forest canopy that protected hillside soil from erosion. No single biological event had a greater impact on mountain subsistence.
details.source: Davis, Donald Edward. 'Where There Are Mountains: An Environmental History of the Southern Appalachians.' University of Georgia Press, 2000, pp. 175-195.

details.description: Mountain women's food preservation work was the margin between sufficiency and hunger. A competent farm woman dried 50-100 pounds of leather-britches beans (strung on thread and hung from rafters), canned 200-400 jars of vegetables and fruit, salted and smoked 200-400 pounds of pork, dried bushels of apples, and stored potatoes, turnips, and cabbage in a root cellar. This labor, performed in addition to daily cooking, cleaning, child-rearing, and garden work, was rarely counted as 'work' in census records, which listed these women as 'keeping house.' The preservation economy was invisible in official records but essential to family survival.
details.source: Inscoe, John C., ed. 'Appalachians and Race: The Mountain South from Slavery to Segregation.' University Press of Kentucky, 2001, pp. 185-200.

details.description: By 1910-1920, many mountain families operated in two economies simultaneously. The wife and children maintained the farm — planting, hoeing, preserving, tending livestock — while the husband worked for wages in a coal mine, sawmill, or timber operation. He might be gone for weeks or months, sending cash home for the goods the farm could not produce: salt, coffee, sugar, ammunition, cloth, and increasingly, commercial flour and canned goods. The family's survival depended on both income streams, and the loss of either — a mine closure or a crop failure — could trigger a crisis. Census records from this period often capture the duality: the husband's occupation is 'miner' while the family's address is a rural route in the same county.
details.source: Eller, Ronald D. 'Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers,' pp. 86-110.

economic_trajectory.narrative: The Appalachian subsistence squeeze was a declining trajectory — not a sudden crash but a generational slide from fragile sufficiency to inadequacy. The first generation in this pattern (1880-1900) was poor but self-sufficient: they owned their land, fed themselves, and needed little cash. The second generation (1900-1920) found the farm insufficient and began supplementing with wage work, entering a dependency they had not known before. The third generation (1920-1940) faced the full choice: stay on a farm that could no longer support a family, or leave for the mines or the cities. The economic trajectory was not dramatic — no single year of catastrophe — but the cumulative effect across three generations was the transformation of independent subsistence farmers into dependent wage workers, whether in Appalachian coal camps or Midwestern factories.

details.description: The mathematics of partible inheritance drove the squeeze with relentless precision. A 100-acre hollow farm divided among four sons in 1860 produced four 25-acre holdings. Each of those, divided among three sons in 1890, produced holdings of about 8 acres. At 8 acres of steep hillside, a family could grow corn for bread and keep a garden, but it could not produce a surplus, graze enough livestock to sell, or feed itself through a bad year. Deed books in eastern Kentucky and West Virginia counties show this subdivision pattern with documentary clarity — the same family name appearing on ever-smaller parcels across three generations of probate records.
details.source: Eller, Ronald D. 'Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers,' pp. 15-25.

details.description: The arrival of timber and coal companies transformed the mountain economy but left most mountain families poorer. Timber companies bought millions of acres of ridgeline forest, logged them bare, and moved on — leaving eroded hillsides, silted streams, and families who had lost their open range. Coal companies acquired mineral rights through 'broad form deeds' that gave them the right to mine beneath a family's farm, build roads across it, and dump waste on it — while the family technically still 'owned' the surface. The wages these companies paid were real, but they replaced a subsistence economy with a cash dependency that left families vulnerable to layoffs, mine closures, and company-store price manipulation.
details.source: Caudill, Harry M. 'Night Comes to the Cumberlands: A Biography of a Depressed Area.' Little, Brown and Company, 1963, pp. 60-90.

details.description: The shift from subsistence self-sufficiency to cash dependency was the central economic transformation of the squeeze. A family that in 1880 produced nearly everything it consumed — food, clothing, shelter materials, tools — by 1920 needed cash for commercial flour, manufactured clothing, school supplies, medical care, and increasingly, taxes assessed on land whose value was rising due to mineral deposits the family could not access. The need for cash drove men into wage work; the wage work drew them away from the farm; the farm declined without their labor; the declining farm produced even less, requiring even more cash. The cycle was self-reinforcing.
details.source: Shapiro, Henry D. 'Appalachia on Our Mind: The Southern Mountains and Mountaineers in the American Consciousness, 1870-1920.' University of North Carolina Press, 1978, pp. 85-115.

family_dynamics.narrative: The squeeze reshaped mountain family structure over three generations. The grandparent generation (married in the 1860s-1870s) lived the yeoman pattern — large families, everyone on the farm, the patriarch directing labor and the matriarch managing the household economy. The parent generation (married 1890s-1900s) experienced the split: fathers gone to the mines for part of the year, mothers running the farm effectively as single parents for months at a time, children divided between farm chores and the new county schools. The grandchild generation (born 1910-1925) grew up knowing that the farm was not enough and that their future lay elsewhere — in the coal camps, the factory cities, or the military. The family's emotional center remained the home place long after it ceased to be an economic center, and the pattern of 'going home' for holidays and funerals persisted for generations after the last family member left the hollow.

details.item: Women as de facto farm managers
details.description: As men left for wage work, mountain women became the primary managers of the farm economy. They made planting decisions, directed children's labor, managed livestock, handled preservation and food storage, and negotiated with store merchants. Census records list them as 'keeping house,' but their actual role was closer to farm manager and sole operator. This invisible authority shaped a generation of mountain women who were far more economically competent than the 'barefoot mountain wife' stereotype suggested. Their labor held the home place together during the decades when men's labor was sold to coal and timber companies.
details.source: Anglin, Mary K. 'Women, Power, and Dissent in the Hills of Carolina.' University of Illinois Press, 2002, pp. 35-60.

details.description: The squeeze era coincided with the expansion of public schooling in Appalachia, creating a tension between the farm's labor needs and the children's future prospects. Keeping a child in school meant losing a worker in the field or the garden. Sending a child to school meant investing in a future that would almost certainly carry that child away from the hollow. Mountain families made this calculation consciously: some pulled children out after the sixth or eighth grade to work; others sacrificed farm productivity to keep a child in school through high school. Settlement schools and mission schools (Hindman, Pine Mountain, Berea) offered boarding education that physically removed children from the farm economy, accelerating the squeeze.
details.source: Whisnant, David E. 'All That Is Native and Fine: The Politics of Culture in an American Region.' University of North Carolina Press, 1983, pp. 30-60.

details.description: When mountain children left the hollow, they did not leave randomly. They followed kinship networks — a cousin in Dayton, an uncle in Detroit, a brother-in-law in Akron. These networks provided initial housing (often a spare room or a couch), job referrals (the cousin's foreman was hiring), and social support in an unfamiliar urban environment. The result was chain migration that reproduced hollow community structures in industrial cities: families from the same Kentucky hollow clustered in the same Cincinnati neighborhood, attended the same church, and married each other's children. The kinship network was the migration's infrastructure, and its density determined where each family member landed.
details.source: Obermiller, Phillip J., and Thomas E. Wagner. 'African American Miners and Migrants: The Eastern Kentucky Social Club.' University of Illinois Press, 2004, pp. 15-40.

record_signatures.description: Deed books in Appalachian counties record the progressive subdivision of family tracts across generations. A single entry in the 1820s — a 200-acre grant to a patriarch — is followed by probate divisions in the 1850s, further subdivisions in the 1880s, and finally parcels of 5-15 acres in the 1910s. Mineral rights transfers and broad-form deeds appear alongside family divisions, documenting the dual squeeze of subdivision and extraction.
record_signatures.genealogical_value: Tracks the economic trajectory of the family through land area. Subdivision patterns reveal inheritance customs and family size. Mineral rights sales identify which families sold subsurface rights and when — often a marker of economic desperation.

record_signatures.description: Federal census records from 1880 through 1940 document the occupational shift across three generations: grandfather listed as 'farmer' in 1880, father as 'farmer' or 'miner' in 1910, son as 'miner' or 'factory laborer' in 1930. The 1940 census, with its supplemental questions about income and weeks worked, is particularly valuable for documenting the dual-economy family.
record_signatures.genealogical_value: Tracks the family's economic transition from subsistence agriculture to wage labor. Comparing consecutive censuses for the same family reveals the squeeze in real time. Birthplace data for children can reveal periods of residence in coal-camp or city locations.

record_signatures.description: Where they survive, coal company records document employment dates, wage rates, housing assignments, and company-store purchases. Collections at the Eastern Kentucky University Archives, the West Virginia State Archives, and various county historical societies hold scattered company records. These records are uneven in survival but extremely detailed when available.
record_signatures.genealogical_value: Establishes the specific dates and locations of wage employment, filling gaps between census records. Company-store ledgers document family purchases and economic condition. Housing records reveal which families lived in company housing versus maintaining separate farm residences.

record_signatures.description: School enrollment records, teacher reports, and attendance registers document children's education — and the seasonal patterns that reveal the squeeze. Enrollment dropping every spring (planting) and fall (harvest) indicates a family still farming. Year-round enrollment indicates a family that has shifted to wage work. School records also document the moment when a family's children appear at a coal-camp school rather than a hollow school, marking the transition to company-town residence.
record_signatures.genealogical_value: Fills gaps in vital records and traces family movement between farm and company-town locations. Attendance patterns reveal the family's economic priorities — a child pulled from school for farm work is evidence of the squeeze's pressure.

record_signatures.record_type: Old Regular Baptist and Missionary Baptist church records
record_signatures.description: Mountain churches maintained membership rolls, baptismal records, and minutes of monthly business meetings. Church minutes record membership transfers — 'letters of dismission' granted when a member moved to another congregation — providing precise dates of family movement. Baptismal records document family relationships, and funeral records fill gaps in civil death registration, which was spotty in rural Appalachia until the 1920s.
record_signatures.genealogical_value: Documents vital events (births, marriages, deaths) not recorded in civil registers. Letters of dismission provide specific dates and destinations for family movements. Church discipline records reveal community tensions and individual circumstances that no other record type captures.

cultural_markers.marker: Multi-generational presence in a single hollow or watershed
cultural_markers.description: Census records showing the same surname in the same district across four or five consecutive censuses (1860-1940) are the primary marker of the Appalachian subsistence pattern. The persistence is itself the story — decades of rootedness in a place where the land was thin but the attachment was deep. When the surname finally disappears from the census, the hollow's chapter is closing.

cultural_markers.marker: Occupational shift across generations in census records
cultural_markers.description: The grandfather listed as 'farmer,' the father as 'farmer and miner,' the son as 'miner' — this three-generation occupational shift, visible in consecutive census records, is the documentary fingerprint of the squeeze. Each step away from 'farmer' marks a step deeper into wage dependency and a step closer to departure.

cultural_markers.marker: Family cemetery on the home place
cultural_markers.description: Mountain family cemeteries, often on a hillside above the home site, contain the most complete record of the family's tenure. Headstones spanning 1810-1940 tell the story of arrival, stability, and departure. The cemetery's maintenance — mowed and decorated on Memorial Day, or abandoned and overgrown — indicates whether the family's connection to the place survives the departure.

Creeks, hollows, ridges, and gaps named for families document long tenure on the land. 'Smith Hollow,' 'Jones Creek,' 'Baker Gap' — these place names, appearing in deeds, topographic maps, and local usage, persist long after the families themselves have left. A USGS topographic map of any eastern Kentucky or West Virginia county is a directory of former residents.

narrative_hooks: Five generations of the {family_name} family had farmed that hollow in {origin_county}, but by {decade} the farm had been halved with every funeral, and there was no longer enough hillside to feed the children it produced.

The timber company bought the ridgeline, the chestnut blight killed the mast trees, and the coal company offered wages — each blow struck the family's self-sufficiency, and by {year}, {ancestor_name} was working underground for the cash the farm could no longer provide.

When {ancestor_name} left {origin_county} for {destination_city}, {pronoun_subject} likely followed a cousin or a brother who had gone first — because mountain families did not scatter randomly but flowed along kinship lines, reconstituting the hollow's community in a factory neighborhood three hundred miles away.

{ancestor_name}'s children left {origin_county} the way the hollow emptied — not in a flood but in a slow drain, one at a time, first to the coal camps and then to the cities, until the farm held only the old couple and the graves.

The land in {origin_county} probably looked like freedom to the first generation and like a prison to the fourth — too little acreage to feed a family, too much history to leave without grief, and no choice that didn't cost something the family couldn't afford to lose.

In {destination_city}, the {family_name} family likely lived among neighbors from the same county they had left — because the factory neighborhoods of Ohio and Michigan were, in many ways, Appalachian hollows rebuilt in brick and asphalt, held together by the same kinship bonds that had held them together on the mountain.

counter_narratives.myth: Appalachian families were isolated, backward people who failed to modernize and were left behind by progress.
counter_narratives.reality: Mountain families were not isolated by choice or ignorance but by geography and deliberate economic extraction. Timber and coal companies extracted billions of dollars in wealth from Appalachia while leaving the population impoverished. The families who stayed were making rational decisions based on attachment to land, kin, and community. The families who left demonstrated remarkable adaptability, transitioning from subsistence agriculture to industrial wage work within a single generation.
counter_narratives.source: Eller, Ronald D. 'Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers,' pp. xv-xxv.

counter_narratives.myth: Mountain people were too proud or stubborn to leave and missed their chance at prosperity.
counter_narratives.reality: Millions of Appalachian families did leave, constituting one of the largest internal migrations in American history. Between 1940 and 1970, roughly 3.5 million people left Appalachia for industrial cities. Those who stayed often did so to maintain family land, care for elderly parents, or preserve community institutions. The staying was as deliberate as the leaving, and neither was a sign of character failure.
counter_narratives.source: Obermiller, Phillip J., Thomas E. Wagner, and E. Bruce Tucker, eds. 'Appalachian Odyssey: Historical Perspectives on the Great Migration.' Praeger, 2000, pp. 1-20.

counter_narratives.myth: The subsistence squeeze was caused by mountain families having too many children for the land to support — a Malthusian overpopulation problem.
counter_narratives.reality: Large families were an economic strategy, not a failure of planning. In a subsistence economy, children were labor — the only labor a family could afford. The squeeze was caused not by too many children but by the enclosure of the commons (timber company purchases), the destruction of the chestnut (blight), the extraction of mineral wealth without adequate compensation (broad-form deeds), and an inheritance system that subdivided land without creating alternatives. The population pressure was real, but it was the result of structural forces, not reproductive irresponsibility.
counter_narratives.source: Davis, Donald Edward. 'Where There Are Mountains,' pp. 175-195.

source_references: Eller, Ronald D. 'Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers: Industrialization of the Appalachian South, 1880-1930.' University of Tennessee Press, 1982.
Caudill, Harry M. 'Night Comes to the Cumberlands: A Biography of a Depressed Area.' Little, Brown and Company, 1963.
Davis, Donald Edward. 'Where There Are Mountains: An Environmental History of the Southern Appalachians.' University of Georgia Press, 2000.
Shapiro, Henry D. 'Appalachia on Our Mind: The Southern Mountains and Mountaineers in the American Consciousness, 1870-1920.' University of North Carolina Press, 1978.
Obermiller, Phillip J., Thomas E. Wagner, and E. Bruce Tucker, eds. 'Appalachian Odyssey: Historical Perspectives on the Great Migration.' Praeger, 2000.
